food_subgroup: Lentil-based stew with vegetables and meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Dalca exists; closest defensible match is lentil-based stew food product (FOODON_03315671). Dalca is a Malay-Indian hybrid dish combining chana dal, vegetables, and meat bones.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for cooked lentil dish used as closest defensible match. Dalca includes additional vegetables and meat bones not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dalca or a sufficiently close lentil stew equivalent. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Dalca is approximately 250 g, typically ladled alongside a plate of Nasi Briyani or Nasi Minyak at a Malay or Indian-Muslim meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Lentil-based dishes and pulse stews
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Dalca entry found in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Values are adapted from HPB entries for cooked chana dal, mixed vegetable curry, and meat-bone broth components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for lentils (NDB 16069) and mixed vegetable stew. Input AI-generated values at confidence 0.9 were retained where consistent with these references. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g per 100 g based on proportion of animal fat from bone broth and cooking oil in analogous HPB curry entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — lentil and pulse dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Dalca found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 estimated by analogy with cooked chana dal (GI ~28–36, University of Sydney GI Database) and mixed lentil-vegetable curry (GI ~41–52, Atkinson et al. 2008). The addition of potatoes and tamarind raises the effective GI slightly above plain dal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 14.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 16.65, rounded to 16.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg per 100 g; potassium 320 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Dalca contains a moderate sodium level of 280 mg per 100 g (700 mg per standard 250 g serving), which approaches one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and reduced-salt preparation are advised, while the high potassium content (320 mg per 100 g) provides a partially offsetting vasodilatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45), high dietary fibre (3.5 g per 100 g), moderate protein (6.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dalca has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and provides 3.5 g of dietary fibre per 100 g from lentils and vegetables, supporting slow glucose absorption and improved postprandial glycaemic control; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake per serving (approximately 37 g per 250 g serving) and limit added potato portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from bone broth and cooking oil; beneficial fibre and potassium from lentils and vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dalca provides beneficial dietary fibre (3.5 g per 100 g) and potassium (320 mg per 100 g) that support cardiovascular health, but the use of mutton or beef bones contributes saturated fat (estimated 1.8 g per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lean-bone or vegetable-only preparations and monitor total serving size to limit saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin); nightshade vegetables (eggplant, potato)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and cumin in Dalca provide curcumin and antioxidant compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should request preparations without eggplant or potato.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dalca (also spelled Dalcha) is a rich, thick, and savory lentil-based stew that occupies a unique culinary position at the intersection of Malay and South Indian cooking traditions. It is a staple accompaniment to fragrant rice dishes such as Nasi Briyani and Nasi Minyak in Malaysia and Singapore, particularly at Malay-Indian Muslim (Mamak) celebrations and festive gatherings. The foundation of the dish is chana dal (split yellow chickpea lentils), which are simmered until completely soft and thick, creating a naturally creamy, protein- and fibre-rich base. A variety of vegetables — most commonly potatoes, carrots, eggplant (brinjal), and okra — are added to the lentil base, contributing texture, colour, and micronutrients including vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and iron. Mutton or beef bones, often with marrow and small attached meat pieces, are traditionally boiled with the lentils to impart deep umami richness and a gelatinous body to the stew. The spice profile is complex and aromatic: turmeric provides colour and anti-inflammatory curcumin; coriander and cumin add earthy warmth; ginger and garlic form the pungent base; and a touch of tamarind introduces a mild, pleasant tang that balances the richness. The finished stew is hearty, wholesome, and nutritionally balanced, providing approximately 125 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 5.5 g fat, and 14.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45. Its high dietary fibre content (3.5 g per 100 g) supports glycaemic control and digestive health, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion sizes are monitored. The moderate sodium content (280 mg per 100 g) requires attention for hypertensive individuals. Dalca is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is typically served in 250 g portions alongside rice.
common_user_queries: What lentils are used in Dalca?
What is the difference between Dalca and dal curry?
meal_context: Served as an accompaniment to Nasi Briyani
Served as an accompaniment to Nasi Minyak

confidence_reason: No exact Dalca entry was found in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB cooked lentil and mixed vegetable curry entries and USDA SR Legacy lentil data, cross-referenced with the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate based on chana dal and mixed lentil-vegetable curry entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat is estimated. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g per 100 g based on proportion of animal fat from bone broth and cooking oil in analogous HPB curry entries; no direct measurement available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Dalca as a composite traditional preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 3. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dalca exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 14.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 16.6 g for a 250 g serving. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for Dalca exists in current public releases. 6. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE (retinol activity equivalents) estimated from beta-carotene content of vegetables; original input value of 85.0 mcg retained. 7. Folate reported as mcg DFE estimated from lentil and vegetable components; original input value of 45.0 mcg retained.
